title: Unit 1: Fundamentals of Cyber Security
description: The bedrock of digital safety. Understand the 'Why' and 'How' of cybersecurity.

modules.title: Introduction to Cyber Security
modules.description: Foundational concepts, the CIA triad, and why everyone is a target.

cards.title: What is Cyber Security?
cards.body: It is the art of protecting networks, devices, and data from unauthorized access or criminal use. In the physical world, we have locks and guards; in the digital world, we have cybersecurity.

cards.title: The Scale of the Problem
cards.body: Every 39 seconds, a hacker attack occurs somewhere in the world. With billions of devices connected, the 'attack surface' is practically infinite. Your phone is a doorway into your entire life.

cards.title: Why it's Important
cards.body: It protects personal data like passwords and photos, prevents identity theft and financial fraud, and ensures critical systems like banking and healthcare run smoothly.

quiz.question: What is the primary goal of cybersecurity?
options: To protect systems and data from digital attacks
quiz.explanation: Cybersecurity focuses on defending computers, servers, mobile devices, electronic systems, networks, and data from malicious attacks.

cards.title: Confidentiality
cards.body: Keeping information private. Only authorized people should access it. Tools: Strong passwords, Encryption, and 2FA. Example: Your email should only be accessible by you.

cards.title: Integrity
cards.body: Ensuring data is correct and untampered. Data should not be changed by unauthorized users. Example: Your exam marks or bank balance should not be altered by hackers.

cards.title: Availability
cards.body: Systems and data must be accessible when needed. Tools: Backups and maintenance. Example: Online banking and ATMs should be available 24/7.

quiz.question: Which pillar of the CIA Triad is violated if a hacker changes the grades in a university database?
quiz.explanation: Integrity ensures data is accurate and untampered. Changing data without authorization is a direct violation of integrity.

modules.title: Types of Cyber Security
modules.description: From networks to your pocket: the different domains of protection.

cards.title: Network Security
cards.body: Protects internet connections from unauthorized access. Tools: Firewalls (digital borders), VPNs (secure tunnels), and IDS (Intrusion Detection Systems).

cards.title: Cloud Security
cards.body: Protects data stored online (Google Drive, AWS). It allows access from anywhere but requires strong passwords and 2FA to prevent exposure.

quiz.question: What acts as a digital border to block unauthorized traffic into a network?
quiz.explanation: A firewall monitors and filters incoming and outgoing network traffic based on established security policies.

cards.title: Application Security
cards.body: Focuses on protecting apps. Includes secure design, regular updates, and bug fixing. Tip: Only download apps from trusted stores like Google Play.

cards.title: Information (Data) Security
cards.body: Protects data from being accessed or changed. Uses Encryption (making data unreadable) and Access Control (restricting who can see what).

quiz.question: What is the process of converting data into a secret, unreadable code called?
quiz.explanation: Encryption makes data unreadable to anyone who doesn't have the key, protecting it from unauthorized access.

cards.title: Endpoint & IoT Security
cards.body: Endpoint protects devices (phones, laptops) using Antivirus. IoT Security protects smart devices (TVs, watches). Tip: Always change default passwords on smart home devices.

cards.title: Operational Security (OpSec)
cards.body: How organizations manage data internally. Includes rules for access and employee training. Tip: Avoid sharing sensitive info publicly in daily life.

quiz.question: Which of these is a critical first step for securing a new Smart TV or IoT device?
quiz.explanation: Hackers can easily find default passwords for smart devices. Changing them is the first line of defense.

modules.title: Hackers vs Ethical Hackers
modules.description: Understanding the mindset: the good, the bad, and the grey.

cards.title: Black Hat Hackers
cards.body: Criminals who break into systems for illegal purposes like stealing data or money. They are the 'villains' who exploit vulnerabilities.

cards.title: White Hat (Ethical) Hackers
cards.body: The 'heroes'. They use the same skills as Black Hats but with permission and for good. They find weaknesses so they can be fixed.

cards.title: Grey Hat Hackers
cards.body: They live in the middle. They might hack a system without permission to show the owner the weakness, but they don't do it to steal.

quiz.question: A hacker who exposes a vulnerability to a company without their permission, but without malicious intent, is called a:
quiz.explanation: Grey Hats operate without formal authorization but usually don't have the malicious intent of a Black Hat.

cards.title: Beginners & Learners
cards.body: Script Kiddies use pre-made tools without deep knowledge. Green Hat Hackers are new learners actively trying to gain skills.

cards.title: Nation-State & Hacktivists
cards.body: State-Sponsored hackers work for governments for spying. Hacktivists hack systems to promote political or social causes.

cards.title: The Defenders
cards.body: Red Hat Hackers aggressively target black-hats to stop them. Blue Hat Hackers are security pros invited to test software before it's released.

quiz.question: Which type of hacker is motivated by political or social causes?
quiz.explanation: Hacktivists use hacking as a form of activism to bring attention to social or political issues.

modules.title: Why Students are Vulnerable
modules.description: The 'Perfect Storm' of access and inexperience.

cards.title: Valuable Access
cards.body: Students aren't just targets; they are keys. Their credentials grant access to University Wi-Fi, VPNs, and massive research databases worth millions.

cards.title: PII Goldmines
cards.body: Student records contain Names, SSNs, addresses, and financial data (FAFSA). This information is highly valuable for identity theft and tax fraud.

quiz.question: Why are student accounts considered 'gateways' for hackers?
options: Students have a lot of money
They provide access to university networks and research
Students always have the latest tech
They use the internet more than adults
quiz.explanation: A student's login can give an attacker a legitimate foothold inside a massive, high-value university network.

cards.title: Pirated Software
cards.body: To save money, students often download 'cracked' software or free streaming tools. These are classic vectors for malware and keyloggers.

cards.title: Unsecured Public Wi-Fi
cards.body: Connecting from coffee shops without a VPN allows attackers to perform Man-in-the-Middle (MitM) attacks to intercept your traffic.

quiz.question: What is a common danger of using pirated or 'cracked' software?
options: It often contains hidden malware or backdoors
It takes up too much RAM
quiz.explanation: Hackers frequently hide malware inside free/pirated software to infect users who are looking for a bargain.

cards.title: MFA & Password Managers
cards.body: Enable Multi-Factor Authentication (MFA) on all accounts. Use a password manager like Bitwarden to avoid reusing passwords.

cards.title: If You Are Hacked
cards.body: 1. Turn off internet immediately. 2. Enable/Check your Firewall. 3. Change all passwords from a different, clean device. 4. Report it to your IT department.

quiz.question: What is the very first thing you should do if you suspect your device is hacked?
options: Change your password on that device
quiz.explanation: Disconnecting the internet stops the hacker from further accessing your data or controlling your device.

modules.title: Real-life Case Studies
modules.description: Learning from history's biggest cyber crimes.

cards.title: WannaCry (2017)
cards.body: A global attack that infected 200,000+ computers. It used a Windows vulnerability to lock files for Bitcoin ransom, crippling UK hospitals.

cards.title: The Equifax Breach
cards.body: Personal data of 147 million people stolen because of an unpatched web vulnerability. It led to a $700 million fine and massive identity theft risks.

quiz.question: What was the main lesson from the WannaCry attack?
options: Always update software and apply security patches
quiz.explanation: WannaCry spread using a vulnerability in older Windows systems that had already received a security patch, but many systems hadn't updated.

cards.title: The Aadhaar Leak (India)
cards.body: A vulnerability allowed access to the private data of over 1 billion citizens. It highlighted the risk of centralized national databases.

cards.title: Cambridge Analytica
cards.body: A quiz app on Facebook harvested data of 87 million users for political profiling. It showed that 'free' apps often trade your privacy for data.

quiz.question: How did Cambridge Analytica obtain user data?
quiz.explanation: They used a personality quiz app that gathered not just the user's data, but also data from their entire friend list.

cards.title: Jamtara Phishing (India)
cards.body: A small village became a phishing capital, stealing OTPs by pretending to be bank officers. It's a prime example of successful social engineering.

cards.title: The Uber Cover-up
cards.body: Hackers stole data of 57 million users. Uber paid $100,000 to hide it instead of reporting. Transparency and reporting are essential after a hack.

quiz.question: What was the main method used in the Jamtara scams?
quiz.explanation: Jamtara scammers used social engineering and phone calls to trick people into giving up their sensitive bank details.

title: Unit 2: Linux Basics for Cyber Security
description: Mastering the preferred operating system of security professionals.

modules.title: OS Fundamentals & Linux Importance
modules.description: The foundation of computing and why Linux rules the security world.

cards.title: The Intermediary
cards.body: An OS acts as a bridge between the user and the computer hardware. It manages resources like CPU, memory, and storage, making it easier to run applications.

cards.title: Core Functions
cards.body: The OS handles: 1. Security (Firewalls), 2. Memory Management, 3. Process Management (Multitasking), and 4. Device/File Management.

cards.title: Types of OS
cards.body: Modern systems include Multi-user (Linux/Windows), Real-Time (Robotics/Medical), and Mobile (Android/iOS). Each is optimized for specific tasks.

quiz.question: Which type of OS is designed to execute tasks within a guaranteed, strict time limit?
quiz.explanation: RTOS is used in mission-critical systems like medical devices and air traffic control where timing is everything.

cards.title: Open Source Transparency
cards.body: Since the source code is public, security experts can audit it for backdoors. Patches for vulnerabilities are often released within days, not months.

cards.title: Granular Permissions
cards.body: Linux follows a strict permission model (Owner/Group/Others). Malware can't easily install itself system-wide because system files require 'Root' (Sudo) access.

cards.title: Native Security Tools
cards.body: Over 90% of security tools (Nmap, Wireshark, Metasploit) are Linux-native. They require low-level system access that Windows often restricts.

quiz.question: Why is 'Open Source' considered a security advantage for Linux?
options: Anyone can audit the code for vulnerabilities
quiz.explanation: Transparency allows the global security community to find and fix bugs much faster than proprietary software companies.

modules.title: Kali Linux Overview
modules.description: Introduction to the industry-standard OS for penetration testing.

cards.title: What is Kali Linux?
cards.body: Maintained by Offensive Security, it's a Debian-based distribution pre-loaded with hundreds of tools for pentesting, forensics, and reverse engineering.

cards.title: Key Features
cards.body: 1. 600+ Security Tools. 2. Custom Kernel (supports Wi-Fi injection). 3. 'Undercover Mode' to look like Windows. 4. Live Boot (runs from USB without trace).

quiz.question: What is the primary purpose of 'Kali Undercover' mode?
options: To make the desktop look like Windows
quiz.explanation: Undercover mode helps security pros blend in when working in public places by mimicking a standard Windows environment.

modules.title: Linux File System & Navigation
modules.description: Learning the tree structure and the power of the command line.

cards.title: Everything is a File
cards.body: In Linux, everything starts at '/' (Root). '/home' is for users, '/etc' for config files, and '/var/log' is where the system logs its activity.

cards.title: Important Folders
cards.body: 1. /bin: Basic commands (ls, cp). 2. /etc: Password/Network settings. 3. /var: Changing data (logs). 4. /root: The superuser's home.

quiz.question: Which directory contains system configuration files like network settings and passwords?
quiz.explanation: /etc is the central location for all system-wide configuration files in Linux.

cards.title: Navigating & Viewing
cards.body: 'ls' lists files. 'cd' changes directory. 'pwd' shows your current path. 'cat' prints the content of a file to your screen.

cards.title: Managing Files
cards.body: 'mkdir' creates folders. 'touch' creates empty files. 'cp' copies, 'mv' moves/renames, and 'rm' deletes. Use 'rm -r' to delete folders.

quiz.question: Which command tells you exactly what type of data a file contains (regardless of its extension)?
quiz.explanation: The 'file' command looks at the file's internal header to determine its true type, helping detect hidden malware.

modules.title: Permissions & User Management
modules.description: Mastering access control and the power of the Root user.

cards.title: The Superuser (Root)
cards.body: Root has total control. It can delete anything and access any file. Standard users are restricted to their /home folder for safety.

cards.title: Sudo (Superuser Do)
cards.body: Instead of staying logged in as root (risky!), users use 'sudo' to run specific commands with root privileges. This is safer and logs every action.

quiz.question: What is the safest way to perform administrative tasks in Linux?
quiz.explanation: Sudo allows temporary elevated privileges without the extreme risk of staying logged in as the all-powerful Root user.

cards.title: Read, Write, Execute
cards.body: Permissions are set for Owner, Group, and Others. 'r' (read=4), 'w' (write=2), 'x' (execute=1). Together they form a 3-digit number (e.g., 755).

cards.title: Chmod & Chown
cards.body: 'chmod' changes file permissions (like chmod 700 for privacy). 'chown' changes the owner of a file. These are vital for securing data.

quiz.question: What does the numeric permission '755' mean?
options: Owner has full (7), Group/Others can Read/Execute (5)
No one can read the file
quiz.explanation: 7 (4+2+1) means rwx. 5 (4+1) means r-x. So Owner has full, others can read/run but not modify.

modules.title: Software & System Logs
modules.description: Installing tools and tracking every move on the system.

cards.title: What is APT?
cards.body: Advanced Package Tool. It handles installing and updating software from trusted 'Repositories' (online app stores for Linux).

cards.title: Core Commands
cards.body: 'sudo apt update' refreshes the list. 'sudo apt install <name>' adds software. 'sudo apt upgrade' installs latest security patches.

quiz.question: Which command should you run first before installing any new software via APT?
quiz.explanation: 'apt update' synchronizes your local list of packages with the online servers to ensure you get the latest version.

cards.title: The Digital Trail
cards.body: Logs are the history of the system. Found in /var/log/. 'auth.log' tracks login attempts. 'syslog' records general events.

cards.title: Live Monitoring
cards.body: 'tail -f /var/log/auth.log' shows login attempts as they happen. This is how security pros spot brute-force attacks in real-time.

quiz.question: Which log file is most important for investigating unauthorized login attempts?
quiz.explanation: auth.log (or secure log in some systems) tracks every authentication attempt, success or failure.

title: Unit 3: Security Concepts & Principles
description: Deep dive into the CIA Triad and the pillars of digital trust.

modules.title: Confidentiality & Access Control
modules.description: Ensuring only authorized eyes see your data.

cards.title: Password Protection
cards.body: The first line of defense. A secret known only to the user to prove identity. Example: Online banking requires a strong password to hide your balance from others.

cards.title: Encryption
cards.body: Scrambling data so it's unreadable without a key. Encryption 'at rest' protects stored files, while encryption 'in transit' (like HTTPS/TLS) protects data moving over the web.

quiz.question: Which mechanism protects your data while it travels over public Wi-Fi?
quiz.explanation: Encryption in transit (like TLS/HTTPS) ensures that even if traffic is intercepted, the content remains unreadable.

cards.title: DAC (Discretionary)
cards.body: Least restrictive. The file owner decides who has access. Example: In Linux, you use 'chmod' to give a friend read access to your document.

cards.title: MAC (Mandatory)
cards.body: Most restrictive. The system enforces access based on security labels (Top Secret, Secret). Common in military and government systems.

cards.title: RBAC (Role-Based)
cards.body: Permissions are tied to roles, not people. Example: A 'Manager' role can see salaries, but an 'Intern' role cannot. Very popular in businesses.

quiz.question: In which access control model does the system owner define fixed parameters that users cannot bypass?
quiz.explanation: Mandatory Access Control (MAC) is the most restrictive; it uses system-enforced labels that users cannot alter.

modules.title: Integrity & Availability
modules.description: Keeping data accurate and services running.

cards.title: Hashing & Checksums
cards.body: Hashing (SHA-256) creates a unique 'fingerprint' for a file. If even one bit changes, the hash changes completely. This proves the file hasn't been tampered with.

cards.title: Digital Signatures
cards.body: Combines hashing with encryption. It proves two things: the sender's identity and that the message hasn't been changed since it was signed.

quiz.question: What is the primary use of a 'Checksum' in networking?
options: To detect errors or tampering in data packets
quiz.explanation: Checksums are used for error detection. If the computed checksum doesn't match the one in the packet, the data is considered corrupted.

cards.title: Redundancy & Backups
cards.body: The 3-2-1 Rule: 3 copies of data, 2 different media types, 1 copy offsite. This ensures you can recover from ransomware or hardware failure.

cards.title: Load Balancing & DDoS Defense
cards.body: Load balancers spread traffic across multiple servers to prevent overload. DDoS mitigation (like Cloudflare) filters out junk traffic designed to crash your site.

quiz.question: Which of these is a direct attack on 'Availability'?
quiz.explanation: A Distributed Denial of Service (DDoS) attack floods a server with traffic to make it unavailable to legitimate users.

modules.title: Advanced Authentication
modules.description: Modern ways to prove who you are.

cards.title: Biometrics & Tokens
cards.body: Biometrics use 'something you are' (fingerprint, FaceID). Security tokens (like YubiKey) are 'something you have'. These are much harder to steal than passwords.

cards.title: Single Sign-On (SSO)
cards.body: One login to access everything. It reduces 'password fatigue' but creates a 'single point of failure' if the main account is hacked.

quiz.question: What is a major security risk of Single Sign-On (SSO)?
options: Users forget their passwords more often
If the central account is compromised, all connected apps are at risk
quiz.explanation: Since SSO grants access to many services with one login, that single account becomes a high-value target for hackers.

cards.title: What is CAPTCHA?
cards.body: It stands for 'Completely Automated Public Turing test to tell Computers and Humans Apart'. It blocks bots from spamming or brute-forcing login pages.

cards.title: Verification Codes
cards.body: Risk-based security. If you log in from a new city, the app asks for a code sent to your phone. This stops hackers who have your password but not your device.

quiz.question: What does an 'Invisible CAPTCHA' monitor to tell if you are human?
options: Behavior patterns like mouse movements and typing speed
quiz.explanation: Invisible CAPTCHAs use background signals like mouse randomness and scrolling patterns to detect bots without annoying users.

modules.title: The Art of Non-Repudiation
modules.description: Making it impossible to deny an action.

cards.title: What is Non-Repudiation?
cards.body: Ensuring that someone cannot deny an action they performed (like sending an email or making a payment). It's the 'digital receipt' that holds up in court.

cards.title: Transaction Logs
cards.body: Immutable records of 'Who, What, When, Where'. In high-security systems, these logs are 'hashed' and chained so they cannot be edited later.

quiz.question: Which of these provides the strongest technical proof for non-repudiation?
options: A typed name at the end of a document
quiz.explanation: Digital signatures using PKI provide mathematical proof of intent and identity that is very difficult to legally deny.

cards.title: Public Key Infrastructure (PKI)
cards.body: The system that manages digital certificates. It's like a 'digital ID office' that verifies your identity and gives you a private key for signing.

cards.title: E-Sign vs Digital Signature
cards.body: An E-sign is just a typed name or checkbox (weak). A Digital Signature uses advanced math to link your identity to the document content (strong).

quiz.question: What is the role of a Certificate Authority (CA) in PKI?
options: To act as a trusted third party that issues digital IDs
quiz.explanation: A CA (like DigiCert) verifies your identity and issues a digital certificate, acting as the 'Root of Trust' for the internet.
